step2_rag_chunks: Bidi smoke is more concentrated than cigarette smoke and delivers higher levels of nicotine despite being thinner. This means bidi dependence can be as strong as or stronger than cigarette dependence, and withdrawal symptoms can be equally intense. Standard cessation support â€” behavioral strategies and nicotine replacement â€” is just as applicable and effective for bidi smokers.

A craving is not a command â€” it is a wave. It builds, peaks, and fades, usually within 3 to 5 minutes. Urge surfing means observing the craving without acting on it â€” noticing where you feel it in your body, watching it get stronger, and then watching it pass. Each time you surf an urge without smoking, it gets slightly easier the next time.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

triggers: after a tough day of work

step2_rag_chunks: Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.

step2_rag_chunks: Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
Keeping a diary of triggers and cravings can help identify patterns and develop better coping strategies.

There are two kinds of triggers â€” situational and emotional. Situational triggers are external: after meals, with tea, during a work break. Emotional triggers are internal: stress, boredom, loneliness, anxiety. Most people have both. Knowing which type is stronger for you helps us choose the right approach.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Support from friends, family, or healthcare professionals can improve the chances of successfully quitting smoking.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

A relapse does not erase the progress made. The most useful question after a slip is not why did I fail but what was happening just before â€” what was the trigger, what was the situation. That specific information is what makes the next attempt more informed. Most people who successfully quit smoked again at some point during the process.

- Motivation to quit: family pressure, weight concerns, health concerns, I've never done anything hard in my life
- Known triggers: social situations, after a tough day of work
[Clinical Knowledge]
- Support from friends, family, or healthcare professionals can improve the chances of successfully quitting smoking.

- If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
- If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.
[Recent Conversation]

Therapist: It sounds like you're considering how to approach your brother without putting any pressure on him. Can you imagine what you would say to him if you were to have this conversation, and how you think he might respond?

Patient: Yeah, I can imagine it. I'd just tell him what the doctor said, and how I'm feeling, and see how he reacts. He'll probably just tell me to stop smoking again, but if he's listening, maybe he'll actually say something useful this time.

Therapist: It sounds like you're thinking about how to approach your brother in a way that feels natural and non-pushy. What do you think would be your response if someone, like a friend or coworker, offered you a cigarette in a social situation, like at a party or while taking a break at work?

Patient: I'd probably just say no, or change the subject. It wouldn't be easy, but I don't know, maybe if I've been doing this for a while, I'd be brave enough to just say no and not feel like I'm missing out or anything.
Therapist: You've thought about how to respond to a social situation. That's a big step. Can you imagine yourself saying "no" to a cigarette and feeling confident and proud of your decision?
Patient: Yeah, maybe I can. I mean, it's not like I've never done anything hard in my life. I just moved to the city a few years ago, that was tough. Quitting smoking can't be that different, right?
Current patient message:
Yeah, maybe I can. I mean, it's not like I've never done anything hard in my life. I just moved to the city a few years ago, that was tough. Quitting smoking can't be that different, right?

step7_response: You're starting to see that you've overcome tough challenges before, like moving to the city. How did you prepare for that move, and what skills or strengths did you use to get through it?

turns.user_message: Uh, I just kind of winged it, to be honest. I mean, I saved up some money, found a place to stay, and started looking for a job. I guess I just used my common sense and stuff. It wasn't that hard, but at the time, it felt like a big deal.
step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.
